{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance appraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Upper Upper" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Upper, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Upper, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial approval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

'''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days

Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance appraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Upper Upper" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Upper, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Upper, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial approval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}} [[Category:Ritsuryō system of Japan]] [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Merit Evaluation--> [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Merit Evaluation--> [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:pp]][[category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Merit Evaluation--> [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq deprecated open]]<!--Merit Evaluation--> [[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[sn:erit Evaluation]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:erit Evaluation]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Merit Evaluation]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:考課]]

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance appraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Upper Upper" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Upper, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Upper, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial approval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance appraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Upper Upper" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Upper, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Upper, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|qqth month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|qq月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial approval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance aqqraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.

'''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years

Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).

Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.

Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|qqth month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|qq月1日}}. {{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance aqqraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Uqqer Uqqer" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|qqth month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|qq月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial aqqroval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance aqqraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Uqqer Uqqer" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|qqth month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|qq月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial aqqroval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance aqqraisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "Uqqer Uqqer" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: - '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. - '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (Uqqer, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|qqth month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|qq月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial aqqroval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

Evaluation]][[Category:qq]]

Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

'''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
