{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year }} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year }} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year }} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year }} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year }} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year }} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year }} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year }} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year }} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year }} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year }} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year }} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft}}.

'''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft}}.

After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=draft}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=draft}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=draft}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=draft}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=draft}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=draft}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=draft}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=draft}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=draft}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=draft}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=draft}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=draft}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days

In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|12=draft|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|12=draft|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|12=draft|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft|12=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=draft|12=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=draft|12=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=draft|12=draft}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft|12=draft}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=draft|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=draft|12=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=draft|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=draft|12=draft}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=draft|12=draft}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft|12=draft}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|12=draft|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|12=draft|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|12=draft|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|12=draft|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=Kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=draft|12=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|12=draft|12=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=draft|12=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|12=draft|12=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=draft|12=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|12=draft|12=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=draft|12=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=draft|12=draft}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=draft|12=draft}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=draft|12=draft}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=draft|12=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=draft|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=draft|12=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=draft|12=draft}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=draft|12=draft}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=draft|12=draft}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=draft|12=draft}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=draft|12=draft}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|12=draft|12=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=draft|12=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|12=draft|12=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|12=draft|12=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|12=draft|12=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|12=draft|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|12=draft|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

revision.text:
{{Otheruses|律令制における官人の評価制度|企業の人事考課制度|人事評価}} '''考課'''（こうか）とは、[[律令制]]の[[官人]]における[[勤務評定]]のことである。
== 概要 == 日本における考課制度は[[天武天皇]]の時代に始まったとされ、[[飛鳥浄御原令]]では毎年の考課の結果によって[[叙位]]が実施されていたが、[[大宝律令|大宝令]]の考仕令では身分などに基づいて規定の年数分の考課の成果を叙位に反映させる方式に変更された。この方式に基づく叙位を選叙（せんじょ）と称し、選叙の要件を満たすことを成選（じょうせん、せいせん）、そのために要する規定年数を選限（せんげん）と呼ぶ。なお、[[養老令]]では考仕令は[[考課令]]と[[選叙令]]に分離されている。
考課の対象になる官人は4つの集団に分けられる。 #内長上（ないちょうじょう）－[[四等官]]（[[郡司]]除く）・[[品官]]および、[[内舎人]]・[[五位]]以上の[[散位]] #内分番（ないぶんばん）－中央官司の[[史生]]・[[雑任]]・[[舎人]]・[[兵衛 (官職)|兵衛]]および、六位以下の散位 #外長上（げちょうじょう）－四等官を除く[[国衙]]の官人および、[[軍毅]]・郡司 #外分番（げぶんばん）－地方在住の散位、当初は外散位（げさんい）と称される
考課の対象になるには、毎年[[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]から1年間のうちに一定の上日（じょうじつ）、すなわち勤務日数を必要とし、長上は240日、分番は140日、舎人は200日の上日を満たす必要があった。更に大宝令以後には成選・選限の制度が導入され、内長上は6年、内分番は8年、外長上は10年、外散位（外分番）は12年分の選限を満たす必要があったが、[[慶雲]]3年（[[706年]]）に2年ずつ短縮され、それぞれ4年・6年・8年・10年とされた。なお、養老律令編纂時に慶雲の選限改定が反映されなかったために、同律令が施行された[[天平宝字]]元年（757年）に一旦旧制に復帰したが、天平宝字8年（[[764年]]）に元に戻された。
毎年、各[[官司]]の長（外長上・外分番は所属国の[[国司]]）8月末までに上日の要件を満たした者の勤務状況を審査して評定原案を作成し、各官人に読み聞かせた上で、官司単位で報告書である考文（こうぶん）を作成する。内長上は上上・上中・上下・中上・中中・中下・下上・下中・下下の9段階評価が行われた。この際に評定の基準となったのは、「善」と「最」である。善とは四善とも称された[[儒教]]的[[価値観]]に基づく4つの事項（徳義有聞・清慎顕著・公平可称・恪勤匪懈）のことで選叙の対象となる中中以上になるためにはこの要素を必要とした。最とは職務の達成基準を意味しており、全部で42の項目があった。私罪（公務以外の罪）があれば中下または下下、公罪（公務における罪）があれば下下と評価され、[[解官]]の対象とされた。郡司・軍毅は上・中・下・下下の4段階（下下は解官）、その他の外長上・内分番・外分番は上・中・下の3段階で評価された。
その後、考文と参考資料を[[京官]]と[[畿内]]諸国は[[10月1日 (旧暦)|10月1日]]、その他の諸国は[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]までに文官は[[式部省]]、武官は[[兵部省]]に送付した。ただし、前者は[[和銅]]2年（[[709年]]）に、後者は[[和銅]]6年（[[713年]]）に[[弁官]]が受付、その後両省に送付されることになった。その後、中央にて最終決定が行われるが、六位以下は式部省・兵部省にて、四位・五位は[[太政官]]にて審議の後に天皇の裁可を仰ぎ、三位以上は天皇の勅裁（大臣は上日の日数のみが考課の対象とされる）によって決められた。
成選に至った場合、所属官司・国司は選文を作成し、期間中全てが中中もしくは中であった場合を標準的な評価として1階昇叙させ、平均して中中・中よりもマイナス評価であった場合、昇叙は見送られプラスの範囲が一定に達するごとに昇叙する位階は1階ずつ増えていった（ただし、内分番・外長上・外分番は最大3階まで）。ただし、五位以上の昇叙および成選の結果[[叙爵]]の対象となる（五位以上に至る）昇叙、成選の結果4階以上の昇叙の場合には全て天皇への上奏と裁可を必要としていた。
[[奈良時代]]前期（[[8世紀]]前半）までは考課の諸原則が基本的に履行されていたが、次第に先例重視の傾向が強まり、特に五位以上の昇叙および叙爵については全くの政治的理由で行われるようになった。更に[[平安時代]]中期（[[10世紀]]）以後には六位以下の形骸化が進んだため、制度の実質を喪失した。
== 参考文献 == *[[野村忠夫]]「考課」（『国史大辞典 5』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0） *[[大隅清陽]]「考課」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） *[[寺内浩]]「考課」（『日本歴史大事典 2』（[[小学館]]、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3） *[[荊木美行]]「考課」（『日本古代史大辞典』（[[大和書房]]、2006年） ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7）
{{DEFAULTSORT:こうか}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

{{Otheruses|律令制における官人の評価制度|企業の人事考課制度|人事評価}} '''考課'''（こうか）とは、[[律令制]]の[[官人]]における[[勤務評定]]のことである。
== 概要 == 日本における考課制度は[[天武天皇]]の時代に始まったとされ、[[飛鳥浄御原令]]では毎年の考課の結果によって[[叙位]]が実施されていたが、[[大宝律令|大宝令]]の考仕令では身分などに基づいて規定の年数分の考課の成果を叙位に反映させる方式に変更された。この方式に基づく叙位を選叙（せんじょ）と称し、選叙の要件を満たすことを成選（じょうせん、せいせん）、そのために要する規定年数を選限（せんげん）と呼ぶ。なお、[[養老令]]では考仕令は[[考課令]]と[[選叙令]]に分離されている。
考課の対象になる官人は4つの集団に分けられる。 #内長上（ないちょうじょう）－[[四等官]]（[[郡司]]除く）・[[品官]]および、[[内舎人]]・[[五位]]以上の[[散位]] #内分番（ないぶんばん）－中央官司の[[史生]]・[[雑任]]・[[舎人]]・[[兵衛 (官職)|兵衛]]および、六位以下の散位 #外長上（げちょうじょう）－四等官を除く[[国衙]]の官人および、[[軍毅]]・郡司 #外分番（げぶんばん）－地方在住の散位、当初は外散位（げさんい）と称される
考課の対象になるには、毎年[[8月1日 (旧暦)|8月1日]]から1年間のうちに一定の上日（じょうじつ）、すなわち勤務日数を必要とし、長上は240日、分番は140日、舎人は200日の上日を満たす必要があった。更に大宝令以後には成選・選限の制度が導入され、内長上は6年、内分番は8年、外長上は10年、外散位（外分番）は12年分の選限を満たす必要があったが、[[慶雲]]3年（[[706年]]）に2年ずつ短縮され、それぞれ4年・6年・8年・10年とされた。なお、養老律令編纂時に慶雲の選限改定が反映されなかったために、同律令が施行された[[天平宝字]]元年（757年）に一旦旧制に復帰したが、天平宝字8年（[[764年]]）に元に戻された。
毎年、各[[官司]]の長（外長上・外分番は所属国の[[国司]]）8月末までに上日の要件を満たした者の勤務状況を審査して評定原案を作成し、各官人に読み聞かせた上で、官司単位で報告書である考文（こうぶん）を作成する。内長上は上上・上中・上下・中上・中中・中下・下上・下中・下下の9段階評価が行われた。この際に評定の基準となったのは、「善」と「最」である。善とは四善とも称された[[儒教]]的[[価値観]]に基づく4つの事項（徳義有聞・清慎顕著・公平可称・恪勤匪懈）のことで選叙の対象となる中中以上になるためにはこの要素を必要とした。最とは職務の達成基準を意味しており、全部で42の項目があった。私罪（公務以外の罪）があれば中下または下下、公罪（公務における罪）があれば下下と評価され、[[解官]]の対象とされた。郡司・軍毅は上・中・下・下下の4段階（下下は解官）、その他の外長上・内分番・外分番は上・中・下の3段階で評価された。
その後、考文と参考資料を[[京官]]と[[畿内]]諸国は[[10月1日 (旧暦)|10月1日]]、その他の諸国は[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]までに文官は[[式部省]]、武官は[[兵部省]]に送付した。ただし、前者は[[和銅]]2年（[[709年]]）に、後者は[[和銅]]6年（[[713年]]）に[[弁官]]が受付、その後両省に送付されることになった。その後、中央にて最終決定が行われるが、六位以下は式部省・兵部省にて、四位・五位は[[太政官]]にて審議の後に天皇の裁可を仰ぎ、三位以上は天皇の勅裁（大臣は上日の日数のみが考課の対象とされる）によって決められた。
成選に至った場合、所属官司・国司は選文を作成し、期間中全てが中中もしくは中であった場合を標準的な評価として1階昇叙させ、平均して中中・中よりもマイナス評価であった場合、昇叙は見送られプラスの範囲が一定に達するごとに昇叙する位階は1階ずつ増えていった（ただし、内分番・外長上・外分番は最大3階まで）。ただし、五位以上の昇叙および成選の結果[[叙爵]]の対象となる（五位以上に至る）昇叙、成選の結果4階以上の昇叙の場合には全て天皇への上奏と裁可を必要としていた。
[[奈良時代]]前期（[[8世紀]]前半）までは考課の諸原則が基本的に履行されていたが、次第に先例重視の傾向が強まり、特に五位以上の昇叙および叙爵については全くの政治的理由で行われるようになった。更に[[平安時代]]中期（[[10世紀]]）以後には六位以下の形骸化が進んだため、制度の実質を喪失した。
== 参考文献 == *[[野村忠夫]]「考課」（『国史大辞典 5』（[[吉川弘文館]]、1985年） ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0） *[[大隅清陽]]「考課」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） *[[寺内浩]]「考課」（『日本歴史大事典 2』（[[小学館]]、2000年） ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3） *[[荊木美行]]「考課」（『日本古代史大辞典』（[[大和書房]]、2006年） ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7）

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=Kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|12=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days

A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|12=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|12=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|12=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|12=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|12=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|12=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|12=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

}}

{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=Kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|qq=draft}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|ja|飛鳥浄御原令|qq=draft}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|qq=draft}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|ja|大宝律令|qq=draft}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|qq=draft}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|qq=draft}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|qq=draft}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|qq=draft}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|qq=draft}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|qq=draft}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|qq=draft}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|qq=draft}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|qq=draft}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|qq=draft}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|qq=draft}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|qq=draft}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|qq=draft}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|qq=draft}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|8月1日|qq=draft}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3rd year|qq=draft}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|qq=draft}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8th year|qq=draft}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|8th month|ja|8月|qq=draft}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi|ja|国司|qq=draft}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|qq=draft}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|ja|儒教|qq=draft}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|qq=draft}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|qq=draft}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|qq=draft}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|qq=draft}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|qq=draft}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|qq=draft}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|qq=draft}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|qq=draft}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|qq=draft}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|qq=draft}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|qq=draft}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{citation needed|Kōka}}

By {{ill|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|10月1日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|qq=}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|ja|式部省|1=Shikibu-shō|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikibu-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikibu-shō|qq=}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Hyōbu-shō|ja|兵部省|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|lt=Ministry of War|qq= (pre-modern Japan)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōbu-shō|13=User:Immanuelle/Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|qq=}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|ja|11月1日|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|qq=}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2nd year|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|qq=}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|qq=}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|ja|太政官|1=Daijō-kan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijō-kan|qq=}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nara period|qq=}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|qq=}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|qq=}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|qq=}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|qq=}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{translated page|ja|考課|version=102607129|comment=Imported full ja history}}
