<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610689]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. Under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Kōka Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Senjo Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on [[:en:August 1|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late [[:en:August|8th month]], the heads of each office (or [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By [[:en:October 1|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]], Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]] (for civil officials) or [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]] (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by [[:en:November 1|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

'''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on [[:en:August 1|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late [[:en:August|8th month]], the heads of each office (or [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By [[:en:October 1|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]], Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]] (for civil officials) or [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]] (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by [[:en:November 1|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{translated page|ja|考課|version=102607129|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:考課]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610689]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]] in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. Under the [[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]], promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the [[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Kōka Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Senjo Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on [[:en:August 1|8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late [[:en:August|8th month]], the heads of each office (or [[:en:Kokushi (official)|Kokushi]] for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]], necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By [[:en:October 1|10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]], Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the [[:en:Shikibu-shō|Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs]] (for civil officials) or [[:en:Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|Ministry of War]] (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by [[:en:November 1|11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)]]. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the [[:en:Daijō-kan|Daijō-kan]] with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]], kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|WD=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years

Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|WD=Q122|lt=8th month}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|WD=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11610689]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Kōka Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Senjo Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|WD=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|WD=Q122|lt=8th month}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|WD=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]]
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Kōka Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Senjo Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=Five Ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Gungi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|WD=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|WD=Q122|lt=8th month}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Kaikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|WD=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{translated page|ja|考課|version=102607129|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:考課]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]]
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|WD=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q335181}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|WD=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Q65279068|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Q65285114|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=75 dharmas in five ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Q11637430|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|WD=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=August 1}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|WD=Q122|lt=8th month|1=August}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Q11630916|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=October 1}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|WD=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=November 1}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=75 dharmas in five ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Q11637430|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|WD=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=August 1}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|WD=Q122|lt=8th month|1=August}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|WD=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|WD=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Q11630916|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|WD=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=October 1}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|WD=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|WD=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|WD=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=November 1}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|WD=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.
{{translated page|ja|考課|version=102607129|comment=Imported full ja history}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:考課]]
{{Otheruses|Evaluation system for officials under the Ritsuryō system|Personnel evaluation systems in companies|Performance a11raisal}} '''Kōka''' (考課) refers to the performance evaluation system for {{ill|officials|ja|官人|1=kanjin|2=ja|3=官人|4=ko|5=관인|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452075}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō|en|Ritsuryō|qid=Q742460|lt=Ritsuryō system|1=Ritsuryō}} in Japan.
== Overview == The kōka system in Japan is believed to have originated during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu|2=ja|3=天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Tenmu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q335181}}. Under the {{ill|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|en|Asuka Kiyomihara Code|qid=Q2981422|lt=Asuka Kiyomihara Code|1=Asuka Kiyomihara Code}}, promotions ({{ill|Joi|ja|叙位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Joi|1=court rank bestowal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11410715}}) were conducted annually based on kōka results. However, the {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code|1=Taihō Code}} introduced a revised method under the {{ill|Kōshi Code|ja|考仕令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshi Code|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, where promotions reflected cumulative performance over a specified number of years, determined by Rank and position. This promotion system was termed {{ill|Senjo|ja|選叙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with those meeting its requirements called {{ill|Jōsen|ja|成選|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōsen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the required time frame referred to as {{ill|Sengen|ja|選限|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 養老律令|1=Yōrō Code|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2575743}} later divided the Kōshi Code into the {{ill|Kōka Code|ja|考課令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōka Code|1=Q65279068|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65279068}} and the {{ill|Senjo Code|ja|選叙令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senjo Code|1=Q65285114|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q65285114}}.
Officials subject to kōka were divided into four categories: 1. '''Naichōjō (内長上)''': {{ill|Shitōkan|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} (excluding {{ill|Gunji|ja|郡司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gunji|qq=}}), {{ill|Hinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinkan|qq=}}, {{ill|Udoneri|ja|内舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Udoneri|qq=}}, and dispersed officials Ranked {{ill|Fifth Rank|ja|五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fifth Rank|1=75 dharmas in five ranks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q903222}} or higher. 2. '''Naibunban (内分番)''': Clerks ({{ill|Shisei|ja|史生|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisei|1=shishō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11412126}}), {{ill|Miscellaneous officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452075}}, {{ill|Toneri|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toneri|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q126081127}}, {{ill|Hyōe|ja|兵衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hyōe|1=Hyōe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24897921}}, and dispersed officials below the Sixth Rank. 3. '''Gechōjō (外長上)''': Officials at {{ill|Kokuga|ja|国衙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokuga|1=kokuga|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11421342}} excluding Shitōkan, as well as {{ill|Gungi|ja|軍毅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gungi|1=Q11637430|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11637430}} and Gunji. 4. '''Gebunban (外分番)''': Locally residing dispersed officials, initially termed {{ill|Gesani|ja|外散位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gesani|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
To be eligible for evaluation, officials had to meet certain attendance requirements over the year starting on {{ill|August 1|en|August 1|qid=Q2788|lt=8th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=August 1}}: - Naichōjō: 240 days - Naibunban: 140 days - Toneri: 200 days
After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|qid=Q122|lt=8th month|1=August}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Q11630916|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|qid=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=October 1}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|qid=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|qid=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|qid=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=November 1}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.

By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
== References == * Nomura Tadao, "Kōka," in {{ill|Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja|国史大辞典 5|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten 5|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1985), ISBN 978-4-642-00505-0. * Ōsumi Kiyoharu, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Heibonsha, 1993), ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1. * Terauchi Hiroshi, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja|日本歴史大事典 2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Rekishi Daijiten 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Shogakukan, 2000), ISBN 978-4-09-523002-3. * Hanaki Yoshiyuki, "Kōka," in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yamato Shobō, 2006), ISBN 978-4-479-84065-7.

After the introduction of the Senjo and Sengen systems under the Taihō Code, the timeframes for Sengen were initially set as follows: - Naichōjō: 6 years - Naibunban: 8 years - Gechōjō: 10 years - Gebunban: 12 years
In {{ill|Keiun 3|ja|慶雲3年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keiun 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (706), these periods were reduced by two years each, to 4, 6, 8, and 10 years, respectively. Although the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} temporarily reverted to the original limits upon its implementation in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja|天平宝字元年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 1|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (757), the revised limits were reinstated in {{ill|Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja|天平宝字8年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpyō-hōji 8|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (764).
Annually, by late {{ill|August|en|August|qid=Q122|lt=8th month|1=August}}, the heads of each office (or {{ill|Kokushi (official)|en|Kokushi (official)|qid=Q1351234|lt=Kokushi|1=Kokushi (official)}} for Gechōjō and Gebunban) evaluated eligible officials and prepared a draft assessment, which was then shared with the officials. A report, known as the {{ill|Kōbun|ja|考文|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, was compiled for submission. Evaluations used a nine-tier scale for Naichōjō, ranging from "U11er U11er" (上上) to "Lower Lower" (下下). Criteria for evaluation included: * '''Zen (善)''': Four virtues rooted in {{ill|Confucianism|en|Confucianism|qid=Q9581|lt=Confucianism|1=Confucianism}}, necessary for middle-tier or higher Rankings. * '''Sai (最)''': 42 performance benchmarks.
Misconduct affected ratings: - Private offenses resulted in "Middle Lower" or "Lower Lower" ratings. - Public offenses resulted in "Lower Lower" and dismissal ({{ill|Kaikan|ja|解官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaikan|1=Q11630916|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11630916}}).
Other categories used simpler scales: - Gunji and Gechōjō: Four levels (U11er, Middle, Lower, Lower Lower). - Naibunban and Gebunban: Three levels (U11er, Middle, Lower).
By {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|qid=Q2913|lt=10th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=October 1}}, Kōbun from central offices and surrounding provinces were sent to the {{ill|Shikibu-shō|en|Shikibu-shō|qid=Q3727885|lt=Ministry of Ceremonial Affairs|1=Shikibu-shō}} (for civil officials) or {{ill|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|en|Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)|qid=Q3276048|lt=Ministry of War|1=Ministry of War (pre-modern Japan)}} (for military officials). Provinces farther away submitted by {{ill|November 1|en|November 1|qid=Q2997|lt=11th month, 1st day (lunisolar)|1=November 1}}. From {{ill|Wadō 2|ja|和銅2年|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wadō 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (709), submissions were handled by the {{ill|Benkankyoku|ja|弁官局|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Benkankyoku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁官|1=benkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3270768}} before forwarding to these ministries.
Final decisions on promotions were made centrally: - Sixth Rank and below: By Shikibu-shō or Hyōbu-shō. - Fourth and Fifth Ranks: By the {{ill|Daijō-kan|en|Daijō-kan|qid=Q1192939|lt=Daijō-kan|1=Daijō-kan}} with imperial a11roval. - Third Rank and above: By imperial decree.
When meeting Senjo criteria, officials advanced according to average performance ratings. Exceeding the standard "Middle Middle" (中中) rating could lead to additional Rank increments (up to three for Naibunban, Gechōjō, and Gebunban). Higher Ranks required imperial consent.
By the early {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, kōka principles were largely adhered to. However, political considerations began to influence promotions, particularly for higher Ranks. By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, evaluations for lower Ranks had become largely ceremonial, and the system lost its substantive function.
